entry.title: Batch-specific / -neutral available quantity in storage type when picking for supply to produciton
entry.content:
Customer wants to pick whole pallets and the pallets have several batches on them. When setting rounding to storage type or pocess type it will round to want is need to the next batch and that is all. So if there are the batchs and the second batch satifies the order and it rounds to that batch the last batch on the pallet is not requested.

 

We have changed it to Batch Neutral storage type and now it requests everthing on the pallet. My question is can you still use the FEFO rule for stock removal. Some testing has given different results and just not sure if the data is bad,

entry.title: Shaping the Future with Data and AI: SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine and Generative AI Toolkit
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the general availability of two groundbreaking technologies: the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine and the Generative AI Toolkit. These solutions are designed to elevate your business intelligence and AI capabilities, offering enhanced data connectivity, smarter insights, and faster decision-making.

This blog post highlights the features of these new offerings and showcases how they align with SAP’s long-standing commitment to deliver innovations that drive your business forward. More importantly, it’s our way of saying: We’ve kept our promise.

 

SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine: A New Era of Data Connectivity

At SAP TechEd 2024, we introduced a major leap forward in how businesses can manage and navigate their data. The SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine empowers organizations to integrate, query, and leverage their data in entirely new ways by making connections and relationships between data points transparent. The new engine in SAP HANA Cloud enables you to fully harness your data's potential, driving smarter analytics, streamlined data management, and enhanced business results.

The Knowledge Graph Engine unlocks a deeper understanding of your data by capturing and analyzing complex relationships—something traditional databases struggle to do. This feature introduces a semantic layer on top of your data, enabling you to define, connect, and visualize entities across multiple sources. It’s built to handle and optimize data in the triple store format, an efficient method for representing complex, interconnected data, which enables better decision-making and more flexible data models.

Key Features:

  • Informed Decision-Making: Utilizing the Knowledge Graph Engine enables you to extract richer insights from your data, facilitating more accurate and well-informed decisions.
  • SQL and SPARQL Interoperability: The Knowledge Graph Engine will support SPARQL, the specialized query language for knowledge graph data, while tightly integrating it with SQL.
  • Enhanced Data Structure: With RDF and SPARQL's structured framework, your data models gain enhanced logical precision, leading to more dependable and accurate results.
  • Enhanced AI & Analytics: Provides structured context to AI-driven applications, improving decision-making, recommendations, and search accuracy.
  • Automated Reasoning & Inference: Uses built-in logic to infer new relationships from existing data, minimizing manual data maintenance as with the case of relational data models.

Whether you’re looking to enhance your data governance, improve the discoverability of insights, or develop smarter applications, the Knowledge Graph Engine will be a game-changer in how you think about and use data.

 

Generative AI Toolkit: Unlocking Innovation & Productivity

Alongside the Knowledge Graph Engine, we’re also launching the Generative AI Toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud. As the world of AI continues to evolve, our toolkit is designed to make AI more accessible and usable for our customers. The Generative AI Toolkit is a powerful solution designed to accelerate AI-driven development and data analysis. Users can for example, effortlessly query datasets using natural language and receive results through intuitive AI-driven interactions.

Key Features:

  • Open-Source Python Library & Integration: Leverage advanced generative AI capabilities through Python, enabling intuitive development and smooth integration for AI-driven tasks.
  • Natural Language-Driven Analysis & Automated Code Generation: Perform dataframe analysis with natural language queries and automatically generate code based on best practices and business scenarios code templates.
  • Retrieval Augmented Generation & LLMs Integration: Support for RAG with multiple vector stores and integration with large language models (LLMs) via the Generative AI Hub SDK for more accurate and context-aware AI solutions.
  • Unlocking SAP HANA Cloud’s Database AI Engine Capabilities: Utilizing targeted tools to generate machine learning models for scenarios like Time Series Forecasting on presented business data.
  • Streamlined Machine Learning Application Development: Accelerate the infusion of machine learning scenarios, like classification, regression or time series forecasting into CAP applications with agentic, natural language assistance. This allows for a much faster getting-started experience, simplifying the overall embedded AI application development process.

As organizations seek to empower their workforce with smarter, AI-powered tools, the Generative AI Toolkit offers the scalability and flexibility needed to drive innovation, improve decision-making, and accelerate time-to-value across the organization.

 

Keep the Promise

Both the Knowledge Graph Engine and the Generative AI Toolkit were born out of feedback from our customers and years of research into the challenges businesses face in a data-driven world. In our previous announcements and at past SAP TechEd events, we made bold commitments to revolutionize how organizations use data and AI. We’ve delivered on those promises with these two cutting-edge solutions.

In line with our dedication to providing continuous value, we’ve ensured that the Knowledge Graph Engine and Generative AI Toolkit are production-ready, offering you robust, scalable, and secure platforms to build your next-generation applications and analytics.

 

Early Adopter Care Program: Join us on the Journey

As we mark the general availability of these solutions, we also want to extend an invitation to our customers and partners to join our Early Adopter Care (EAC) Programs. These exclusive programs offer personalized support and guidance as you explore and implement the Knowledge Graph Engine or Elasticity in SAP HANA Cloud.

Being part of the EAC programs means you’ll get early access to the latest features, tools, and documentation, as well as direct engagement with SAP experts who can assist you in optimizing the use of these technologies for your specific needs. Additionally, you’ll be able to influence the direction of future updates and enhancements, ensuring that SAP HANA Cloud continues to meet the evolving demands of your business.

How to Get Involved?

Visit the EAC program pages to learn more about how to enroll:

  • Knowledge Graph Engine - EAC Program
  • Elasticity in SAP HANA Cloud - EAC Program

By joining the EAC programs, you will be at the forefront of the next wave of innovation, helping us shape the future of data management and AI.

 

Looking Ahead

I’m incredibly excited about the opportunities these new capabilities bring to the table. From smarter data connectivity to AI-driven productivity, SAP HANA Cloud is setting the stage for a new era of innovation in data and AI.

We believe these advancements will not only help organizations thrive in an increasingly digital world, but also empower individuals across industries to make better, more informed decisions, faster. These technologies will be pivotal in shaping the future of how businesses interact with data, AI, and the world around them.

We’re committed to supporting your digital transformation journey every step of the way, and we can’t wait to see how you leverage these new capabilities to achieve greater success.

Thank you for being part of the SAP community. The journey has only just begun!

entry.title: What configurations/enhancements in SAP EWM we can implement to prioritize warehouse task creation?
entry.content:
Hello SAP EWM Community,

We’re looking to optimize the creation and prioritization of warehouse tasks for high-demand items in our distribution center managed by SAP EWM. Specifically, we aim to reduce the picking time and improve order fulfillment rates for these items.

Key Points:

  1. Our warehouse handles a mix of high- and low-demand items.
  2. Current picking strategies do not adequately prioritize high-demand items, leading to delays.
  3. We utilize standard picking and putaway strategies configured in SAP EWM.

Specific Question:

What configurations or enhancements in SAP EWM can be implemented to prioritize warehouse task creation for high-demand items, ensuring faster picking and improved order fulfillment?

Looking forward to your insights and recommendations!

Best regards

entry.title: Is there a way we can measure how many times we ran out of stock for a maintenance order
entry.content:
Hi,

We are implementing spare parts inventory indicators and one of those is to measure the stockouts.

In other words, how many times we ran out of stock for a part that was required for a maintenance order at the moment the work was to be performed.

We have tried looking for MSPT status on maintenance orders but that only tells us the order does not currently have the parts needed in inventory but the MRP will run and upon reception of the part, it will remove the MSPT status.

But the order could be due a month after the parts were received.  So this is not a stockout for us.  It is just-in-time supply.

What we want to know is if someone tied to get parts from the store and the parts weren't on the shelf.  I kow we can't capture that in SAP, but I thought maybe there is a way we could detect this situation in some way.

I was looking at MD07 the other day and I'm not entirely sure how we could implement such table in BW, or if anything already exists in BW for that kind of situation.

 

Any ideas anyone?  Or is it hopeless?

entry.title: Re: 2 vs 4 Physical CPUs
entry.content:
Setting the max_query_tasks option to 1 disables intra-query parallelism. That may degrade performance if queries could be parallelized but prevented from doing so. It is generally best to run with this options default.

Please see my comment to your original question related to MaxMultiprogrammingLevel.

entry.title: Re: LMS: Is it possible to create an item with an Ongoing...
entry.content:
Hi @mmarceillac 

Just giving you some background:

  • At the item's record > Online Content tab > Settings > Completion tab > the completion statuses available to be chosen at the 'Completion Status' dropdown are only the ones that will grant credit to the users;
  • If your completion status 'Ongoing not-for-credit completion status' hasn't the option 'Credit Given' enabled (https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-learning/managing-sap-successfactors-learning-for-administrators/adding-course-completion-status?locale=en-US), it means this completion status is considered a status to be given when the users fail the content and get an "Incomplete" status for the item.

Now, I think that what your company is trying to achieve can be done with some workarounds:

  • First, enable 'Credit Given' for 'Ongoing not-for-credit completion status';
  • At the item's record, navigate to Online Content tab > Settings > Completion tab and:
    • Select 'Ongoing not-for-credit completion status' as the new Completion Status;
    • Disable any completion settings that might be enabled (such as 'Add to History on Completion of All Content' and 'Add to History on Pass');
    • Click on 'OK' > then on 'Save' > and reassign the item to the users (so they'll be assigned to these updated settings).
  • At the user side, the users should be able to launch the content and take it until the end, and since there are no completion settings configured, LMS will not grant any completion to them and the item will remain in their Learning Assignments.

I hope this helps!

Best regards,

entry.title: How to call Function Import of external OData for fetching PDF in CAPM application
entry.content:
I am developing CAP app on BAS to fetch PDF file of billing document. I am consuming standard OData API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV in CAPM, I am able to get entities data but on using function Import GetPDF, I always get undefined even though terminal shows no error. Please help.

this is the edmx definition of function import.

 

<FunctionImport Name="GetPDF" ReturnType="API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.GetPDFResult" m:HttpMethod="GET" sap:action-for="API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.A_BillingDocumentType"><Parameter Name="BillingDocument" Type="Edm.String" Mode="In" MaxLength="11000"/></FunctionImport>
 
Below is my code
service.js
=======
 
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
 
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
    const {BD} = this.entities;
    const BDService = await cds.connect.to('API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV');
 
    this.on('READ', 'BD', async (req) => {
        return await BDService.run(SELECT.from(BD)); // Successfully connects and returns data from entity
    });

    this.on('getFile',  async (req) => {
        const {docNo} = req.data;
        const parameters = { BillingDocument: docNo };
        const headers = { "Accept" : "application/json"  };
 
        const result = await BDService.send("GetPDF",parameters,headers);
        return result; // undefined sadly, expecting binary pdf string, directly calling api loads PDF data
    });
 
});
 
service.cds
=========
 
using {API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV} from './external/API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.csn';

service BillingDocumentService {

  entity BD as select from API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.A_BillingDocument;

  function getFile(docNo : String) returns API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV.GetPDFResult;
 
}

I have referred below github for using function import

https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-extension-s4-material-availability/blob/main/srv/service.js

Below is documentation

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/03c04db2a7434731b7fe21dca77440da/990f1ce2078f42e4b593d6d060966bd8.html

entry.title: Re: Why the second ZP document is being created with full...
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

Taking your example, here is how it behaves in the system :

ZP1

  • Debit: Vendor Payables Account (e.g., 31xxxx) → $100
  • Credit: Bank Outgoing Clearing Account (e.g., 27xxxx) → $100

This is the standard vendor payment entry.

ZP2

  • Debit: Bank Outgoing Clearing Account → $100
  • Credit: Bank Outgoing Clearing Account → $100

According to your log, this is meant for bank charges, but both debit and credit are equal, which seems odd.

Then:

  • ZP1's $100 credit is cleared against ZP2's $100 debit, leaving ZP2's $100 credit open.

 

Best Practice: Bank Statement Upload + Clearing

Yes, you're on the right track. Here’s how clearing and bank charges should ideally flow:

  1.  Bank Statement Upload (MT940 or CAMT.053)

When the bank statement is uploaded via Manage Incoming/Outgoing Bank Statements app, it should reflect:

  • Actual payment to vendor: $100
  • Bank charges: $5

The statement line from the bank will be:

Payment Amount

Charge

Net Clearing

$100

$5

$105 debit from your bank account

  1.  Automatic Clearing via FEBAN / Reconciliation Rule
  • The $100 credit open item (ZP2) will be matched with the bank statement entry (the bank’s $100 outflow for vendor).
  • The $5 bank charge will post to a GL account like Bank Charges Expense (64xxxx), based on clearing rules or interpretation algorithms (configurable in Bank Account Master or EBS config).

I hope this explanation will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Re: How do i create GL accounts code for a list of prepai...
entry.content:
Hello dear user,

And thank you for asking your question in the SAP Community Q&A blog.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition) with the Fiori UI, the process of creating G/L accounts for prepaid cards (like debit cards) and credit cards depends on how they are used operationally and accounted for. Let’s go through both cases with practical steps.

 

1. Prepaid Cards (e.g., Prepaid Debit Cards) – Asset Accounts

These are usually treated as cash equivalents or short-term assets, depending on their usage:

  • If it's a reloadable prepaid debit card used by employees or for procurement → treat like Petty Cash / Bank-like account.
  • If it's non-reloadable gift cards or expense cards → treat like Prepaid Expense or Other Current Asset.

 

 G/L Account Setup

App: "Manage G/L Account Master Data"

Steps:

  1. Go to Fiori app: Manage G/L Account Master Data
  2. Click Create (or Copy from similar account, like petty cash)
  3. Fill in the details:

Field

Value

G/L Account Type

Balance Sheet

Account Group

Cash/Bank Account OR Current Assets depending on use

Description

Prepaid Debit Card – e.g., “Prepaid Visa Card”

Financial Statement Version Node

Under Cash Equivalents or Prepaid Expenses

Field Status

Based on company policy (e.g., allow Cost Center or not)

Is Bank/Bank Reconciliation Account?

No, unless linked to a house bank (see below)

 

When to Treat it Like a Bank?

If your prepaid card is reloadable and reconciled, and you want to manage it as a Bank Account, you can:

  1. Create a G/L account with Account Type = Cash Account
  2. Then use the “Manage Bank Accounts” app:
    • Create a new House Bank for the card
    • Assign your G/L account to the Bank Account Master

This way, you can reconcile it and use it in payments, like a regular bank.

 

2. Credit Cards – Liability Accounts

Credit cards are typically treated as liabilities, similar to how vendor liabilities or bank overdrafts are handled.

You should not treat them as banks unless:

  • The card is linked to a house bank for payment integration.
  • You’re using credit card reconciliation with third-party interfaces (e.g., Concur, banks).

 

G/L Account Setup for Credit Cards

App: "Manage G/L Account Master Data"

Field

Value

G/L Account Type

Balance Sheet

Account Group

Liabilities (e.g., Other Liabilities or Accounts Payable)

Description

e.g., “Corporate Credit Card – Amex”

Financial Statement Node

Short-Term Liabilities

Is Reconciliation Account

Yes (optional), if mapped to a subledger like a clearing account

Bank Account Link

Optional, only if using house bank structure

 

I hope this explanation will help you answer your question.

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Re: Project Invoice Request is not generated for the Sale...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

The Project Invoice Request is not automatically created by the system, as it happens with Sales Orders which are not Project based.

The Project Invoice Request must be created by the customer. In the Project Invoicing view in the Customer Invoicing work center, you can create and edit project invoice requests for time and expenses incurred on a customer project.

In order to create the PIR, please follow the below steps:

  1. Navigate to the Customer Invoicing workcenter.
  2. Go to the Project Invoicing view -> Sales Order sub view.
  3. Search for a Sales Order document.
  4. Select the Sales Order and click on New.

Here, you can create an Automatic Project Invoice Request (which will automatically fill the data and set up the Invoice Request for you) or a Manual Project Invoice Request (in which you would need to enter all the data manually and change the settings if desired).

Then, you must save the Invoice Request and proceed with the Invoicing Process.

In case you need more information on how Project Invoicing works in the system, please kindly check the Help Portal website, on the following sections:

Project Invoicing Quick Guide

Create a Manual Project Invoice Request

Create an Automatic Project Invoice Request

Best Regards,

Marcelo
SAP Product Support

entry.title: Re: BTE or substitution does not work druing FB70 simulation
entry.content:
Dear user,

Thank you for your precision.

The issue arises because the Business Transaction Event (BTE) 1120 and the GGB1 substitution/user exit are only triggered after saving the document, but not during simulation in FB70. This means that fields like FIPOS, GSBER, and GEBER are not populated during the simulation phase.

Here are the possible solutions to be explored :

1. Use a Different BTE That Triggers in Simulation

BTE 1120 triggers after saving, but you need something that works during simulation.

Check for BTE 00001030 (Process Line Item for Single-Screen Transaction), which is called during document simulation.

 

2. Enhance the Standard Substitution Mechanism (GGB1)

If the substitution rule is only working after saving, try callpoint 2 instead of callpoint 3.

Ensure your substitution rule is marked for "Check" (instead of "Substitution Only After Save").

 

3. Implement an Enhancement Spot (BADI or Implicit Enhancement)

Since FB70 runs simulation in a different execution path, you might need a BADI that works during the simulation phase.

Try BADI: FI_ITEMS_MASS_CHANGE (use it to modify line items before posting).

Alternatively, explore BADI: AC_DOCUMENT and check its methods.

 

4. Debug the Standard Process for Simulation

Put a breakpoint in function module AC_DOCUMENT_CREATE to track how standard SAP processes simulation.

Check which enhancement spots/BTEs are triggered during simulation.

 

5. Check the Root Cause of F5A049 Error

F5A049 is usually related to missing account assignments in Funds Management.

If the missing fields (FIPOS, GSBER, GEBER) cause this error, ensuring they are filled in before simulation is crucial.

 

Conclusion:

Since standard substitution and BTE 1120 are post-processing events, you need to find a method that modifies line items before simulation completes. The best approach is likely BTE 1030 or a relevant BADI like FI_ITEMS_MASS_CHANGE.

 

Let me know if you need more details!

Best regards,

Jeremy

entry.title: Re: Is there a way we can measure how many times we ran o...
entry.content:
First of all - This is a good question. Like most things, it sounds like a simple request but the way to solve this in SAP can get complex. 

"What we want to know is if someone tried to get parts from the store and the parts weren't on the shelf.  I know we can't capture that in SAP" Actually, this is exactly where I would start. I think you want to track every single time Joe went to the counter and couldn't get what he needed, regardless of whether it was planned or unplanned right?  I would  look into creating a quick BTP application for it and do it as a tally. Literally just have someone log every time that this happens (Material number, quantity requested, order number) 

Now - If you don't want this to be done this way , you could have the guy in the counter create a reservation for the order (assuming he's authorized to do so). Then run the material availability check on the order - which would put it on status MSPT. 

Now here comes the fun part. You will have orders in MSPT status which are NOT shortages; and others which are. You would have to compare the change logs of the order itself to check when the order was in status MSPT and what was the actual start date at the time when the order was in MSPT status. You're essentially asking, did I start this order when the system status was MSPT?  This could technically work ... but if you have good planning - it's unlikely to give you meaningful results. If you have a decent planner, she won't start an order for which she has no parts ! She might reschedule the order, or look for a different component that the techs can use. 

I'm probably telling you a lot of what you already know - but it's a good question and something that I'm often asked about by store teams.

entry.title: Re: Is there a way we can measure how many times we ran o...
entry.content:
Hi @Oscar_DC 

You are 100% right to assume I thought of all those things.  I'm actually impressed that the first answer I get, is someone that understands the assignment!!

As you probably already know, getting dates set correctly in Maintenance Orders is one of our battles.  Making sure techs are returning unused parts to the store's inventory is also one of our battles.

Now, your idea of tracking the start and end of MSPT status and try to make sense of it with order dates is something I was thinking about lately.

I mean, simply checking for MSPT occurrences is not enough. We need to establish rules where MSPT is ok and then, maybe, we can assume whatever does not comply to those rules is potential stock shortage situation.

After that, we could validate exceptions detected, adjust the rules and run it again.

Then we can start showing facts to planners as to why dates must be set on operations as quickly as possible and kept up-to-date to avoid those situations.  And we could also use this as a metric that could, in part, explain inventory adjustments and the lack of compliance to the goods issue/return process.

Your answer is refreshing and gave me ideas.  I'll share it here if I ever come up with something that actually works.  I'd be interested to know if you have implemented this with your clients.  Let me know please.

entry.title: Re: Adopting General Display Name
entry.content:
@HarveyXu 

I'm hoping you can guide me in the right direction.  In anticipation of the 1H 2025 release, we have re-enabled General Display Name on both of our production and staging environments. 

We do not have Employee Central.  

The most immediate problem the enabling of General Display Name has for us currently is that, in Learning Administration, when searching for users, the User Name is now configured as "First M. Last" as opposed to "Last, First M." as is preferred.  This is creating some process issues for our Learning administrators.  

What is confusing me (and simultaneously giving me some hope) is that, this reversal of User Name format is only present on our production environment.  On staging, user name still displays in Learning Admin user search as "Last, First M."  

I've run user export files (Admin Center/Manage Users/Export Users/User) and the resulting export files show values in the DISPLAYNAME column for users in both production and in staging.  So, I took this to mean that users in both places have been assigned General Display Name values as described in KBA 3414673. 

❓So, what do I need to configure in production to maintain the preferred User Name search results format in Learning Admin (while General Display Name is enabled)?  It seems to be working the way we want in our staging environment with General Display Name enabled, but not in production. 

For transparency's sake,  Enable adoption of General Display Name (under Admin Center/Company System and Logo Settings) was checked on production first, and on staging about 8 minutes later.  We are now over 24 hours from when those background migration jobs were initiated, and staging still maintains the preferred User Name format in search results while production does not.

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence in the Work of SAP Basis
entry.content:
he advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) is transforming numerous areas of business, and the realm of enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems is no exception. In particular, SAP Basis consultants, who are responsible for the technical administration of the SAP environment, are witnessing how AI can significantly change and improve their daily work. In this blog, we will explore how AI is revolutionizing the tasks of SAP Basis consultants, increasing efficiency, reducing errors, and enabling a more strategic focus.

1. Automation of Repetitive Tasks and Predictive Maintenance

One of the main advantages of AI is its ability to automate repetitive and maintenance tasks. SAP Basis consultants can greatly benefit from this automation, freeing up their time to focus on higher-value activities.

Example: AI can automate the monitoring of SAP systems, detecting and correcting common issues such as configuration errors, performance failures, or database inconsistencies before they impact users. Additionally, through predictive maintenance, AI can foresee when hardware or software failures are likely to occur, allowing consultants to schedule interventions before disruptions happen.

2. System Performance Optimization

AI can analyze large volumes of system performance data in real-time to identify patterns and trends that human consultants might overlook. This enables continuous and proactive optimization of the SAP system’s performance.

Example: Using machine learning algorithms, AI can identify peak load times in the system and automatically adjust resources to balance the workload, ensuring SAP systems run optimally at all times. This is especially useful in environments with significant demand fluctuations, such as during financial closings or major sales events.

3. Enhanced Security and Compliance

Security is a constant concern for SAP Basis consultants. AI can provide an additional layer of protection by continuously monitoring for suspicious activities and identifying potential threats.

Example: AI systems can analyze access and usage patterns to detect anomalous behaviors that might indicate a security breach attempt. If suspicious activity is detected, the system can alert consultants or even take preventive measures automatically, such as blocking accounts or restricting access to certain resources until a review is conducted.

4. Assistance in Problem Resolution

Resolving technical issues in SAP environments can be complex and time-consuming. AI can act as an intelligent assistant, helping diagnose problems and providing solutions based on historical data and failure patterns.

Example: AI-powered virtual assistants can help SAP Basis consultants troubleshoot technical issues by offering real-time suggestions. For instance, if a performance problem is detected, the virtual assistant could suggest specific adjustments to the system configuration or the application of certain patches based on previous solutions to similar problems.

5. Continuous Training and Development

AI can also play a significant role in the training and continuous development of SAP Basis consultants. AI-driven learning platforms can customize training programs based on individual needs and the experience level of each consultant.

Example: An intelligent learning system could recommend specific courses, tutorials, or training modules based on the areas where a consultant needs improvement or the latest updates and new functionalities of the SAP system. This ensures that consultants are always up-to-date with best practices and emerging technologies.

Conclusion

Artificial intelligence is changing how SAP Basis consultants perform their work, providing powerful tools to automate routine tasks, improve system performance, ensure security, facilitate problem resolution, and support continuous professional development. By adopting these technologies, consultants can significantly increase their efficiency and effectiveness, allowing them to focus on strategic tasks that add greater value to their organizations.

In a world where technology and business evolve rapidly, integrating AI into the SAP Basis field is not just a competitive advantage but a necessity to stay ahead and ensure optimal and secure operation of enterprise systems.

Note: This article was created with ChatGPT AI.

Best Regards,

Eli Daniel Ramones

entry.title: Re: Could Race Conditions Occur in This SAP CAP Scenario?
entry.content:
Update: Found the Root Cause – It Was cds.connect.to Causing the Race Condition After further investigation, I found the root cause of the race condition in my SAP CAP service. The issue was due to the use of await cds.connect.to to call an internal service within the same CAP application.

cds.on('served', async ()=> {
  const db = await cds.connect.to('db') // DANGER: will cause race condition !!!
  db.on('before', (req) => console.log(req.event, req.path))
})


Documentation:  https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/get-started/troubleshooting#don-t

entry.title: Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability) - Part 1
entry.content:
As cloud-native applications become more complex and data-intensive, observability into your backend services is critical for maintaining performance, reliability, and scalability. For organizations using PostgreSQL on SAP BTP, some monitoring capabilities are already integrated into the platform and available to be consumed by external users.

This blog is the part-1 of the blog series about Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability).

In this blog post series, we’ll explore how to monitor PostgreSQL on SAP BTP, using built-in tools and SAP Alert Notification Service. We’ll walk through key features, alert types, and how to configure monitoring for actionable insights.

There are 3 ways that you can use to implement monitoring for your instance as an external user:

  1. Instance monitoring via extension API
  2. Instance monitoring via SAP Alert Notification Service events
  3. Instance monitoring using Monitoring Admin user access
Best Practice: 
The combination of all the above approaches will enable to establish a solid foundation for monitoring and observability of PostgreSQL instances on SAP BTP.

1. Instance monitoring via extension API

PostgreSQL on SAP BTP offers an Instance Monitoring API that allows you to retrieve real-time metrics and insights about your PostgreSQL database. 

The extension API allows to retrieve status of an instance and the below metrics:

  • CPU Utilization (%)
  • Memory Utilization (%)
  • Disk Utilization (%)
  • Max Connections (%)

The syntax of the urls to use in the API call are :

  • Status: https://{base-url}/v1/postgresql-db/instances/{id}/status 
  • Metrics: https://{base-url}/v1/postgresql-db/instances/{id}/metrics

For more details on how to use the above API please check the product SAP Help Portal official documentation:

  • Extension API Reference: Instance Monitoring 

To be able to use instance monitoring API, there are 2 initial prerequisites:

  • Cloud Foundry (CF): Request an API token that is needed for authorization in each API call + retrieve or construct the base URL for the APIs call.
  • Kyma: Create a binding/service key for the target instance + retrieve or construct the base URL for the APIs call.
You may refer to the Extension API Reference documentation to learn on how to execute the above steps.

In next section of this blog, we’ll walk through the process of using Postman, a popular API client, to interact with this API, including setting up requests and interpreting responses.

- Cloud Foundry scenario:

1. Request an API token that is needed for authorization in each API call

cf login -a https://api.cf.eu12.hana.ondemand.com
cf oauth-token

2. Retrieve or construct the base URL for the APIs call

Access to the service key of the PostgreSQL on SAP BTP instance and retrieve the api_server parameter that will be used as {base-url} to construct the API url.

The url will be (considering the instance{id} = '1b71d567-1fbe-4967-xxxx-f8ffb5db5020')

https://api-backing-services.eu12.data.services.cloud.sap/v1/postgresql-db/instances/1b71d567-1fbe-4967-xxxx-f8ffb5db5020/status
https://api-backing-services.eu12.data.services.cloud.sap/v1/postgresql-db/instances/1b71d567-1fbe-4967-xxxx-f8ffb5db5020/metrics

3. Request the status and metrics using GET action

Below the example using Postman after adding the API url and the token:

Change the API url to be able to retrieve the metrics and execute the GET action:

The below example of response from the API provides key performance indicators of your PostgreSQL instance. A sample response might look like:

{
    "CPU Utilization":"70.20%",
    "Memory Utilization":"50.43%",
    "Disk Utilization":"80.42%",
    "Max Connections":"60.05%"
}

Regularly monitoring these metrics helps in proactive database management and ensures optimal performance.

Conclusion: Utilizing the Instance Monitoring API allows for efficient and real-time monitoring of your PostgreSQL instances on SAP BTP. By following the steps outlined above, you can set up, execute, and interpret API requests to keep a close watch on your database’s health and performance.

Thanks for reading! Be sure to check out what our product team has shared about PostgreSQL and Redis:

  • PostgreSQL on SAP BTP - Are you looking for official blog posts? Blog INDEX
  • Redis on SAP BTP - INDEX of official blog posts

Regards, 

Antonio

entry.title: Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability) - Part 2
entry.content:
This blog is the part-2 of the blog series about Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability).

In this blog post, we’ll explore how to monitor PostgreSQL on SAP BTP, using alert notification events. 

There are 3 ways that you can use to implement monitoring for your instance as an external user:

  1. Instance monitoring via extension API
  2. Instance monitoring via SAP Alert Notification Service events
  3. Instance monitoring using Monitoring Admin user access
Best Practice: 
The combination of all the above approaches will enable to establish a solid foundation for monitoring and observability of PostgreSQL instances on SAP BTP.

2. Instance monitoring via SAP Alert Notification Service events

PostgreSQL on SAP BTP integrates with SAP Alert Notification Service, which supports real-time events for operational awareness. PostgreSQL on SAP BTP send events to the SAP Alert Notification Service, that makes available a set of predefined events that help you monitor the health and performance of your PostgreSQL instances. By subscribing to these events, you can receive timely alerts and take proactive measures to address potential issues.

Key Events for PostgreSQL Monitoring

The following are some of the critical events, available for PostgreSQL on SAP BTP:

  • CPU Limit: This alert is triggered when the CPU usage of a PostgreSQL instance exceeds recommended operational thresholds. This alert helps detect performance bottlenecks that may impact query processing and application responsiveness.
  • Memory Limit: This alert indicates that the PostgreSQL instance is consuming a high percentage of allocated memory. Excessive memory usage can lead to process termination or degraded database performance.
  • Max Connections: This alert signals that the number of active connections to the database is nearing or hitting its configured maximum. When max connections are exhausted, new clients cannot connect, potentially causing application downtime.
  • Storage Full: The event is triggered to alert you when the disk storage used by your PostgreSQL instance is approaching full capacity. Hitting storage limits may lead to write failures, impacting database availability and data integrity.

Please find above events and limits detail documentation availanle at:

  • 📈 Reference Documentation: SAP BTP PostgreSQL Events

Check how to create and access an SAP Notification Service instance for your BTP Account:

  • https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/alert-notification-service.html 

Configuring Alert Subscriptions

To effectively monitor your PostgreSQL instances, you can set up subscriptions to these events in the SAP Alert Notification Service:

  • Access the SAP Alert Notification Service: Navigate to the SAP BTP cockpit and select the Alert Notification Service from your subscribed services.
  • Create a New Subscription: In the Alert Notification dashboard, create a new subscription and specify the event types relevant to PostgreSQL monitoring.
  • Define Notification Channels: Choose your preferred notification channels, such as email, Slack, or Microsoft Teams, to receive alerts.
  • Set Event Filters: Configure filters to tailor the notifications to your specific needs, ensuring you receive alerts pertinent to your PostgreSQL instances.
  • Activate the Subscription: Save and activate the subscription to start receiving alerts based on the defined criteria.

Below a list of some of the actions/channels that you can select to use for your alerts:

Please refer to the SAP BTP Alert Notification service documentation for the current supported actions.  

Below a generic example of an alert sent via email:

By leveraging these predefined events and configuring appropriate subscriptions, you can enhance the observability of your PostgreSQL instances on SAP BTP, enabling proactive management and swift response to potential issues.

Thanks for reading! Be sure to check out what our product team has shared about PostgreSQL and Redis:

  • PostgreSQL on SAP BTP - Are you looking for official blog posts? Blog INDEX
  • Redis on SAP BTP - INDEX of official blog posts

Regards, 

Antonio

entry.title: Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability) - Part 3
entry.content:
This blog is the part-3 of the blog series about Monitoring PostgreSQL on SAP BTP (observability).

In this blog post, we’ll explore how to monitor PostgreSQL on SAP BTP, via Monitoring Admin user access

There are 3 ways that you can use to implement monitoring for your instance as an external user:

  1. Instance monitoring via extension API
  2. Instance monitoring via SAP Alert Notification Service events
  3. Instance monitoring using Monitoring Admin user access
Best Practice: 
The combination of all the above approaches will enable to establish a solid foundation for monitoring and observability of PostgreSQL instances on SAP BTP.

3. Instance monitoring using Monitoring Admin user access

Monitoring your instance of PostgreSQL on SAP BTP is crucial for maintaining optimal performance and ensuring system reliability. Some monitoring actions are only possible with direct database access, that allows for in-depth monitoring and management.

To minimize security risks, we advise using the recommended above options and limiting direct access to the instance database to cases where it is essential for critical monitoring tasks 

To perform advanced monitoring tasks, you need elevated privileges. SAP BTP enables you to request a temporary ‘instance_monitoring_admin’ user with admin rights, valid for up to 29 days. This user grants the necessary permissions to execute monitoring and administrative tasks.

This section outlines the process of requesting an Admin Monitoring user and highlights some key SQL commands for effective monitoring.

For requesting the admin monitoring user, please follow the steps explained in the below blog post:

  • PostgreSQL - Request an 'admin' user access valid for 'x' days

You may also refer to the official product documentation here.

After requesting you can start using the 'instance-monitoring-admin' user during the validity time.

With the ‘instance_monitoring_admin’ credentials, you can connect to your PostgreSQL on SAP BTP instance using any SQL client, such as pgAdmin. Once connected, you can execute various monitoring tasks.

Below some useful monitoring tasks that you may execute:

Check Active Sessions: Retrieves information about current database connections and activities

SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity;

Monitor Long-Running Queries: Identifies queries running longer than 5 minutes, which may indicate performance issues.

SELECT pid, now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start AS duration, query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'active' AND now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start > interval '5 minutes';

Analyze Index Usage: Evaluates the effectiveness of indexes in query performance.

SELECT relname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read, idx_tup_fetch
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
JOIN pg_index USING (indexrelid)
WHERE indisunique IS FALSE;

Check Table Bloat: Identifies tables consuming excessive space, indicating potential bloat.

SELECT schemaname, relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size,
pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS relation_size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE pg_total_relation_size(relid) > 100000000;

View Locks:  Displays locks that are waiting to be granted, useful for diagnosing blocking issues.

SELECT pid, locktype, relation::regclass, mode, granted
FROM pg_locks
WHERE NOT granted;

Monitoring key performance metrics such as CPU usage, memory consumption, disk utilization, and active connections is essential for maintaining the health and efficiency of your PostgreSQL database on SAP BTP. Using SQL is not the best option, or even not fully possible, for these metrics, we recommend you to check instance monitoring via extension API. Nevertheless by connecting to your database instance you can perform some of these monitoring tasks effectively to help get more insights about performance. 

CPU Usage: While PostgreSQL does not directly expose CPU usage statistics through SQL queries, you can infer high CPU utilization by identifying long-running or resource-intensive queries.

Memory Consumption: To monitor memory usage, focus on identifying queries that consume significant resources, complementary you may check the buffer usage. 

Run the following SQL command to assess shared buffer utilization:

SELECT relname, heap_blks_hit, heap_blks_read,
       heap_blks_hit * 100.0 / (heap_blks_hit + heap_blks_read) AS hit_ratio
FROM pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY hit_ratio DESC;

A low hit ratio may suggest that queries are not efficiently utilizing memory, leading to increased disk I/O.

Disk Utilization: Monitoring disk space helps prevent issues related to insufficient storage. Checking table sizes can give some hints about the utilization of the storage space.

Use the following SQL command to list the sizes of all tables:

SELECT relname AS table_name,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size
FROM pg_catalog.pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC;

This query provides an overview of the disk space used by each table, allowing you to identify tables that consume excessive storage.

You may also find the below blog post relevant to the above topic:

  • PostgreSQL - How to estimate the remaining storage free space?

Active Connections: Monitoring the number of active connections is crucial to ensure the database operates within its capacity.

Execute the following SQL command to count current connections:

SELECT count(*) AS total_connections
FROM pg_stat_activity;

To view connections by state (active, idle, etc.):

SELECT state, count(*) AS count
FROM pg_stat_activity
GROUP BY state;

Utilizing pgAdmin Dashboards

pgAdmin provides a user-friendly interface to interact with your PostgreSQL database, allowing you to execute SQL queries and view system statistics. By utilizing pgAdmin, you can monitor various performance metrics through SQL commands and built-in dashboards.

☝️Please be aware that pgAdmin is not a SAP tool, for support please access here.

pgAdmin offers some graphical dashboards that provide real-time insights into database performance:

  • Server Activity: Displays information about active sessions, locks, and prepared transactions.
  • Dashboard Tab: Provides an overview of server health, including graphs for transactions per second, tuples in/out, and block I/O.

These visual tools complement SQL queries, offering a comprehensive view of your database’s performance.

Thanks for reading! Be sure to check out what our product team has shared about PostgreSQL and Redis:

  • PostgreSQL on SAP BTP - Are you looking for official blog posts? Blog INDEX
  • Redis on SAP BTP - INDEX of official blog posts

Regards, 

Antonio

entry.title: Re: Database logon failed
entry.content:
Here's the entire procedure, in case it helps:

    Public Function _RunReport(ByVal aReportName As String,
                               Optional ByVal aShowProgress As Boolean = False,
                               Optional ByVal aRunType As Integer = RunType.Foreground) As Boolean

        Dim t As Type = MethodBase.GetCurrentMethod().DeclaringType
        Dim sf As New StackFrame()
        Dim mb As MethodBase = sf.GetMethod()
        Dim lsModuleAndMethod As String = t.Name & "." & mb.Name & ": "

        If gbVerbose Then
            If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                Call P_LogProcedure(t.Name, mb.Name)
            Else
                Call P_LogProcedureBackground(t.Name, mb.Name)
            End If
        End If

        Dim liLocation As Integer = 0
        Dim lipk_report As Integer = 0
        Dim lsFileName As String = ""
        Dim lfrmProgress As frmProgress = Nothing
        Dim lsExportPathAndFileName As String = ""

        Try
            Using lclsDatabase As New clsDatabase
                With lclsDatabase
                    liLocation = 1 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                    If .GetReportPK(aReportName, lipk_report, aRunType) Then

                        ' get a data table of groups for report
                        Dim l_dt As New DataTable
                        liLocation = 2 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                        If .GetReportGroupMembers(lipk_report, l_dt, aRunType) Then

                            ' show progress window
                            If aShowProgress Then
                                liLocation = 3 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                lfrmProgress = New frmProgress(0, l_dt.Rows.Count, "")
                            End If

                            If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                                WriteToLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Processing started for " & aReportName, True)
                            Else
                                WriteToBackgroundLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Processing started for " & aReportName)
                            End If

                            Dim lConnectionInfo As New ConnectionInfo
                            With lConnectionInfo
                                .ServerName = gsServer
                                .DatabaseName = gsDatabase
                                .UserID = gsUserID
                                .Password = gsPassword
                                .IntegratedSecurity = False
                            End With

                            ' for each group member
                            For i As Integer = 0 To l_dt.Rows.Count - 1

                                ' show group name being processed in progress window
                                If aShowProgress Then
                                    lfrmProgress.DisplayMessage(l_dt.Rows(i)("group_name"), True)
                                    lfrmProgress.ProgressBarVisible(True)
                                    lfrmProgress.Show()
                                    Application.DoEvents()
                                End If

                                ' write pk_group to the Parms table
                                liLocation = 4 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                .UpdateParms(lipk_report, CInt(l_dt.Rows(i)("fk_group")), aRunType)

                                ' run the report
                                liLocation = 5 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                Using lrptTechnicianExpirations As New rptTechnicianExpirations
                                    With lrptTechnicianExpirations
                                        ' clear saved data
                                        liLocation = 6 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                        .Refresh()

                                        ' override the database connection string info
                                        For Each table As Table In .Database.Tables
                                            With table
                                                .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo = lConnectionInfo
                                                If gbVerbose Then
                                                    WriteToLog("table.ServerName = " & .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo.ServerName, True)
                                                    WriteToLog("table.DatabaseName = " & .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo.DatabaseName, True)
                                                    WriteToLog("table.Password = " & .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo.Password, True)
                                                    WriteToLog("table.UserID = " & .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo.UserID, True)
                                                End If
                                                .ApplyLogOnInfo(.LogOnInfo)
                                            End With
                                        Next table

                                        For Each subrep As ReportDocument In .Subreports
                                            With subrep
                                                For Each table As Table In .Database.Tables
                                                    With table
                                                        .LogOnInfo.ConnectionInfo = lConnectionInfo
                                                        .ApplyLogOnInfo(.LogOnInfo)
                                                    End With
                                                Next table
                                            End With
                                        Next subrep

                                        ' create the report as a pdf - save in MVIRS Manager\Reports\{group name}\yyyy-mm-dd {report_name} {group_name}.pdf
                                        ' get variable of complete .pdf file name
                                        liLocation = 7 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                        lsFileName = Format(Now(), DateFormatYMD) & " " & aReportName & " " & l_dt.Rows(i).Item("group_name") & ".pdf"

                                        ' get variable of complete export directory name
                                        liLocation = 8 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                        Dim lsExportDirectory = GetSetting(Application.ProductName, "frmOptions", "LocalRootDirectory", "")
                                        lsExportDirectory = lsExportDirectory &
                                            "LifeCycle\" & IIf(gbProduction, "prod\", "dev\") & "Reports\" & l_dt.Rows(i).Item("group_name") & "\"
                                        System.IO.Directory.CreateDirectory(lsExportDirectory)

                                        ' export the report
                                        liLocation = 10 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                        lsExportPathAndFileName = lsExportDirectory & lsFileName
                                        If gbVerbose Then
                                            WriteToLog("lsExportPathAndFileName = '" & lsExportPathAndFileName & "'", True)
                                        End If
                                        .ExportToDisk(CrystalDecisions.Shared.ExportFormatType.PortableDocFormat, lsExportPathAndFileName)
                                        liLocation = 11 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                        .Close()

                                        ' e-mail the report
                                        Dim l_dt_group_properties As New DataTable
                                        liLocation = 12 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation &
                                                                                       ", fk_group = " & l_dt.Rows(i).Item("fk_group") &
                                                                                       ", l_dt_group_properties.Rows.Count = " & l_dt_group_properties.Rows.Count, True)
                                        lclsDatabase.GetGroup(l_dt.Rows(i).Item("fk_group"), l_dt_group_properties)
                                        Using lclsEmail As New clsEmail
                                            With lclsEmail
                                                liLocation = 13 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
                                                ._SendMonthlyReport(aReportName, l_dt_group_properties, lsExportPathAndFileName, aRunType)
                                            End With
                                        End Using
                                    End With
                                    If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                                        WriteToLog("e-mailed " & Path.GetFileName(lsExportPathAndFileName), True)   ' one report created and sent
                                    Else
                                        WriteToBackgroundLog("e-mailed " & Path.GetFileName(lsExportPathAndFileName))   ' one report created and sent
                                    End If
                                    If aShowProgress Then
                                        lfrmProgress.UpdateProgress(i + 1)
                                        Application.DoEvents()
                                    End If
                                End Using
                            Next i
                            If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                                WriteToLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Processing complete", True)
                            Else
                                WriteToBackgroundLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Processing complete")
                            End If
                            If aShowProgress Then
                                Wait(2)
                                lfrmProgress.Dispose()
                                MessageBox.Show("Processing complete", "Run Reports")
                            End If
                            Return True
                        Else
                            If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                                WriteToLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Unable to get group members for " & aReportName, True)
                            Else
                                WriteToBackgroundLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "Unable to get group members for " & aReportName)
                            End If
                            If aShowProgress Then
                                lfrmProgress.Dispose()
                                MessageBox.Show("Processing failed")
                            End If
                            Return False
                        End If
                    Else
                        If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                            WriteToLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "GetReportPK failed for " & aReportName, True)
                        Else
                            WriteToBackgroundLog(lsModuleAndMethod & "GetReportPK failed for " & lsFileName)
                        End If
                        If aShowProgress Then
                            lfrmProgress.Dispose()
                            MessageBox.Show("Processing failed")
                        End If
                        Return False
                    End If
                End With
            End Using

        Catch ex As Exception
            liLocation = 14 : If gbVerbose Then WriteToLog("Location " & liLocation, True)
            With System.Reflection.MethodBase.GetCurrentMethod()
                If aRunType = RunType.Foreground Then
                    WriteErrorToLog(ex.ToString, .ReflectedType.Name & "." & .Name)
                Else
                    WriteErrorToBackgroundLog(ex.ToString, .ReflectedType.Name & "." & .Name)
                End If
            End With
            Return False

        Finally
            If Not IsNothing(lfrmProgress) Then
                lfrmProgress.Dispose()
            End If
        End Try
    End Function

entry.title: Re: Database logon failed
entry.content: Make sure the Crystal Reports Runtime for .NET (matching your development version) is installed on that machine. Also, check that the report’s database credentials are being applied programmatically before export—reports that work locally may fail elsewhere if credentials are hardcoded or missing. Finally, verify the target PC has access to Azure SQL (firewall, connection string, and drivers like OLE DB or SQL Native Client).

entry.title: SAP SD IN FOCUS: WHY THE SALES AND DISTRIBUTION MODULE IS MORE IMPORTANT THAN EVER – ESPECIALLY IN C
entry.content:
At a time when companies around the world are faced with rising customer expectations, more complex supply chains, and the need for process automation, the SAP Sales and Distribution (SD) module is once again taking center stage in the ERP landscape. Especially in conjunction with SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), SD is gaining increasing strategic relevance – not only as an operational sales tool, but also as the central interface between customer orders and the physical movement of goods.

What is SAP SD?

SAP SD (Sales and Distribution) maps the processes surrounding quotation creation, order entry, pricing, delivery, invoicing, and credit memos. It is therefore essential for the order-to-cash process and ensures that customer orders are smoothly transferred to delivery and billing.

The change: From classic order processing to process integration

Traditionally, SD was a self-contained module that interacted primarily with FI (financial accounting) and MM (materials management). However, with the introduction of SAP S/4HANAand the greater decoupling and integration of SAP EWM, the architecture is changing—and with it the role of SD in the overall structure.

Why is this important?
Sales processes no longer end with the creation of a delivery – they are directly integrated with logistics processes in the warehouse , which are managed in EWM. This makes SD the hub between customer requirements, availability checks (ATP), warehouse activities, and transport processing.

 

Practical example: Just-in-time delivery in the automotive industry

An international automotive supplier uses SAP SD to manage its customer orders, which often involve short, precisely timed delivery dates.

  1. Order entry in SD
    An OEM orders bumper modules – the SD order contains specific materials, delivery schedules and customer-specific packaging specifications.
  2. Delivery & EWM Integration
    After the order is created, the delivery is automatically generated and transferred to SAP EWM. There, picking begins according to FIFO principles and in compliance with batch requirements.
  3. Warehouse processes in EWM:
    Warehouse personnel are controlled via mobile devices (RF/MDE). EWM organizes picking waves, creates handling units, and ensures punctual shipping.
  4. Confirmation & Invoicing:
    After successful goods dispatch, the confirmation is automatically sent to SD, which completes the delivery. The invoice is then created and sent to the customer.

Result: A fully integrated end-to-end process with real-time transparency – from order to delivery.

 

New requirements for consultants and key users

Increased integration requires not only technical expertise but also a process understanding across module boundaries. SD consultants today should also understand EWM processes , especially when it comes to delivery items , shipping activities, packing instructions , or transport planning .

 

Conclusion

SAP SD is no longer "just" a sales module – it is a central component in intelligent, end-to-end order and delivery processing. Combined with SAP EWM, it opens up new opportunities for automation, optimization, and customer focus. Anyone who wants to be successful in SAP projects today cannot avoid the close integration of both modules.

entry.title: Re: How to copy data from parent node to child node via d...
entry.content:
As you are already using alternative hierachy where H1 is a leaf member, then you can easily use the same hierarchy where A, B, C, D are all leaf member to copy from H1 to A, C, D. 

MEMBERSET [d/Profit_center] = ("A", "C", "D")

DATA() = RESULTLOOKUP([d/Profit_center] = [d/Profit_center].[p/H_parent])
